original_abstract: Hydrogen fuel cells represent a cornerstone of next-generation power sources for the automotive industry, yet their commercial advancement is hindered by the sluggish kinetics of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). To address this, the development of efficient, durable, and cost-effective electrocatalysts is essential. In this work, we present a non-precious dual-metal single-atom catalyst (SAC) derived from metal-organic frameworks, featuring distinct Co-N₄ and Zn-N₄ local structures. The resulting Zn/Co-N-C catalyst demonstrates exceptional ORR activity, characterized by a half-wave potential ($E_{1/2}$) of 0.938 V vs. RHE and remarkable durability, maintaining its performance with a minimal shift ($\Delta E_{1/2} = -8.5$ mV) after 50,000 electrochemical cycles. When integrated into a realistic alkaline fuel cell, the catalyst achieved a record-breaking peak power density of ~1 W cm⁻², the highest reported to date for single-atom catalysts in such systems. We employed *operando* X-ray absorption spectroscopy to resolve the catalytic mechanism, identifying the Co atom within the Co-N₄ architecture as the primary active center. Our findings reveal that an axial oxygenated species binds to this center to form an O$_{ads}$-Co-N₄ moiety during the reaction. Furthermore, potential-dependent microkinetic modeling and core-level shift calculations corroborate our experimental data, offering a deeper look into the oxygen bonding dynamics at the Co-N₄ sites. This study provides a rigorous mechanistic framework for dual-metal SACs and offers a blueprint for the design of high-performance electrocatalysts for renewable energy applications.
humanized_abstract: Hydrogen fuel cells are considered to be one of the major alternatives for vehicle power supply in the future. Their widespread application is, however, restricted due to the slow oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) kinetics at the cathode. This necessitates not only highly active but also long-lasting and economical catalysts. Based on metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), we designed and synthesized a non-precious dual-metal single-atom catalyst (SAC) with different Co-N₄ and Zn-N₄ centers. The as-obtained Zn/Co-N-C SAC showed a high ORR performance of E1/2 at 0.938 V vs RHE along with good stability with a minor cathodic shift of ΔE1/2 = -8.5 mV after 50,000 cycles. The resulting catalyst showed even better performance in an alkaline fuel cell with a peak power density of ~1 W cm⁻², which is a record value for single-atom catalysts and the highest reported value for a SAC-based AFC. Through *operando* X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) studies on the catalytic behavior, it was confirmed that the Co-N₄ centers in the Zn/Co-N-C SAC were responsible for the ORR activity. Furthermore, the axial oxygenated species is expected to be adsorbed at the Co centers to form the catalytically active Oads-Co-N4 species. The potential-dependent microkinetic modeling (MKG) and core-level shift calculations give further insight into the catalytic activity of the Co-N₄ centers in the Zn/Co-N-C SAC. Therefore, the present study gives a clear and straightforward explanation of the mechanism of the single-atom catalysts of the dual-metal type and acts as an example for designing new catalysts with high catalytic performance.
